new_standing_order.label: New standing order
new_standing_order.description: Article reports a newly issued judicial AI order or local rule.

rule_update.label: Rule update
rule_update.description: Article reports an amendment, withdrawal, or clarification of an existing AI rule.

hallucinated_citations.label: Hallucinated citations
hallucinated_citations.description: Reporting on filings with fabricated or AI-hallucinated case citations.

sanctions.label: Sanctions
sanctions.description: Attorneys sanctioned, referred to bar authorities, or otherwise disciplined for AI misuse.

state_court.label: State court
state_court.description: Activity originating in state courts or state bar authorities.

scholarship.label: Scholarship
scholarship.description: Academic or think-tank analysis of judicial AI policy.

bar_guidance.label: Bar guidance
bar_guidance.description: State or local bar association ethics opinions or practice guidance on AI.

policy_debate.label: Policy debate
policy_debate.description: Opinion pieces, policy arguments, or debates about how courts should approach AI.

articles.title: AI ruling prompts warnings from US lawyers: Your chats could be used against you
articles.summary: Federal judge in New York ruled that a former CEO of a bankrupt financial services company could not shield his AI chats from prosecutors pursuing securities fraud charges. Attorneys are now warning that conversations with chatbots like Claude and ChatGPT could be demanded by prosecutors in criminal cases or by litigation adversaries in civil cases.

articles.title: BCC Research Launches Litigators AI, a Platform to Transform Legal Research and Citation Accuracy for Trial Attorneys
articles.summary: New AI platform launched specifically to address citation accuracy concerns for trial attorneys, responding to the growing crisis of AI hallucinations in legal filings and the escalating sanctions imposed by courts.

articles.title: $5K Sanctions for 'Egregious, Repeated, and Ongoing' AI Hallucinations in Self-Represented Litigant's Filings
articles.summary: Federal judge in Illinois sanctioned a self-represented litigant $5,000 for egregious, repeated, and ongoing AI hallucinations in court filings, striking the plaintiff's motion for violating local rules.

articles.title: Courts escalate sanctions as AI hallucinations in legal filings surge in 2026
articles.summary: U.S. courts escalate enforcement against AI-generated errors, with Q1 2026 sanctions totaling at least $145,000. Courts are moving from treating hallucinated citations as embarrassment to treating them as sanctionable misconduct requiring monetary penalties and professional discipline.

articles.title: State Bar Charges Lawyers Over AI-Generated Fake Citations
articles.summary: California State Bar filed charges against two attorneys for AI-generated fake citations and approved probation for a third. Omid Emile Khalifeh faces six counts of misconduct for an April 2025 trademark filing with fabricated citations, while Steven Thomas Romeyn is accused of filing an October 2025 brief with several made-up citations.

articles.title: Tiger King Attorney Sanctioned for Filing Complaint with AI Hallucinations
articles.summary: Attorney sanctioned despite uncertainty about whether errors were from generative AI or counsel's own sloppiness. Court stated the resulting errors and legal misrepresentations are glaring and warranting sanctions, illustrating courts' intolerance for citation errors regardless of origin.

articles.title: The $145,000 Paradox: Courts Punish Lawyers for Using AI While 61% of Federal Judges Use It Themselves
articles.summary: Analysis reveals that 61.6% of responding federal judges report using AI tools in their judicial work, primarily for legal research and document review, creating questions about enforcement standards while judges themselves use the technology. Examines the paradox of judicial AI use versus attorney sanctions.

articles.title: It's Not An AI Hallucination — It's Lazy Editing Of A Human Paralegal
articles.summary: Analysis argues that many cases attributed to AI hallucinations are actually human editing failures, where attorneys or paralegals fail to properly review and correct AI-generated content before filing, shifting the discourse from technological failure to professional responsibility.

articles.title: Sixth Circuit Sanctions Attorneys for Fake Citations – What Does This Mean for Use of AI?
articles.summary: Sixth Circuit imposed $30,000 sanctions against attorneys for submitting fabricated citations. The decision reinforces that attorneys bear full responsibility for citations regardless of the research method used, including AI-assisted research, and signals appellate courts' willingness to impose substantial penalties for citation fraud.

articles.title: Federal Judge Rules AI-Generated Documents Are Not Privileged – A Game-Changer for Legal Strategy
articles.summary: Judge Jed S. Rakoff of the Southern District of New York ruled on February 10, 2026 that documents generated through a public AI platform were not protected by attorney-client privilege or the work product doctrine in United States v. Heppner, concluding that Claude is plainly not an attorney and that privilege only protects discussions with lawyers.

articles.title: The AI Sanction Wave: $145K in Q1 Penalties Signals Courts Have Lost Patience with GenAI Filing Failures
articles.summary: In the first quarter of 2026, U.S. courts imposed at least $145,000 in sanctions for fabricated AI citations. Researcher Damien Charlotin's database tracking AI hallucination cases worldwide crossed 1,300 cases by April 2026, up from just over 700 at the start of 2026, with ten separate courts flagging AI-fabricated filings on a single day.

articles.title: Penalties stack up as AI spreads through the legal system
articles.summary: NPR reports on the escalating pace of AI hallucination cases in courts, with researcher Damien Charlotin noting the pace has become relentless. Bloomberg Law counted 52 court rulings on improper AI use in February 2026 alone.

articles.title: Attorney disciplined for AI-generated fake cases in Phoenix Suns lawsuit
articles.summary: A federal judge disciplined a Valley lawyer after finding court filings in a Phoenix Suns discrimination case included at least 18 made-up legal citations. As punishment, the judge is ordering the attorney to cover some attorneys' fees and must also take additional training on ethical use of AI.

articles.title: DOJ Attorney's AI‑Generated Brief Sparks Sanctions Threat After Pro Se Plaintiff Uncovers Fabricated Quotes
articles.summary: An assistant U.S. attorney in the Eastern District of North Carolina came under fire after a federal judge found that a court filing contained fabricated quotes, misstated case law, and even fake regulatory language. At the show‑cause hearing, the attorney told Judge Numbers that after accidentally overwriting a prior draft, he felt panicked and had AI rewrite the brief, calling the decision the worst of his career.

articles.title: Parade of Horribles: Federal Court in Oregon Surveys Sanctions for AI Fake Citations
articles.summary: Federal court in Oregon surveyed the growing landscape of sanctions for AI-generated fake citations, documenting a parade of horribles in which courts across the country have imposed increasingly severe penalties for attorneys submitting fabricated legal authorities.
